analysis.period_summary: For the week of 2026-04-13 to 2026-04-19, the funnel generated $4,510.63 USD net revenue (ClickBank) across 106 frontend sales, with CPV and ClickBank purchase counts in perfect alignment. The single most critical finding is a near-universal checkout-to-purchase conversion rate collapse: across all materially-sized flows, checkout-to-purchase rates range from 2.70% to 7.49%, all well below the 15% critical threshold, meaning the ClickBank checkout page is the primary revenue leak this week.

funnel_scorecard.commentary: The funnel top-of-funnel and CTA engagement are healthy (landing-to-optin 19–29%, offer-to-checkout 72–86%), but the ClickBank checkout step is critically underperforming across every active flow (2.7–7.5%), resulting in low overall conversion rates (0.46–1.50%). Backend monetization is also weak, with OTO1 at the borderline average/weak threshold and OTO2 well below benchmark.

funnel_leaks.why_it_matters: With 2,296 total checkout clicks this week and a blended ~5% checkout-to-purchase rate, improving to even 15% would roughly triple frontend revenue from the same traffic and email spend.

The v4-v3 offer page loses nearly half its visitors before the checkout CTA click, compared to 78–86% for other v4 variants, costing an estimated 210 additional checkout clicks per 1,951 landing views.
The base /offer path shows 319 offer page views but only 3 checkout clicks (0.94%), suggesting a broken or missing CTA link on this legacy offer page that is leaking re-routed traffic.

The /b landing + /offer/v2 combination has a below-benchmark offer-to-checkout rate of 46.15% with 0 purchases from 6 checkout clicks; sample is small but directionally weak and warrants investigation.

OTO2 (Timeline) at 7.5% take rate is well below the 15% minimum benchmark, and combined OTO2 + downsell reaches only 10.4%, meaning backend monetization from buyers who declined OTO1 is severely underperforming.

upsell_analysis.commentary: OTO1 take rate of 25.5% sits at the borderline weak/average threshold, and the combined OTO1 + downsell acceptance of 32.1% leaves meaningful revenue on the table from 68 buyers who declined both. OTO2 at 7.5% is critically low (benchmark 15%+), suggesting either a sequence issue, mismatch between the offer and buyer intent, or a technical redirect problem that should be audited immediately.

needle_movers.why: All active flows show checkout-to-purchase rates between 2.70% and 7.49%, all critically below the 15% threshold. With 2,296 checkout clicks this week, lifting the blended rate from ~5% to 15% would add approximately 230 additional frontend sales (~$8,510 revenue at $37 AOV) from identical traffic volume.
what: Audit the ClickBank order form for friction: form length, required fields, payment security badges, and mobile UX — check for any recent changes coinciding with the rate drop
Test a price-point reduction or trial offer (e.g. $27 or $17 trial) on a split of checkout clicks to identify whether price resistance is the primary barrier
Add a checkout page bump or pre-checkout bridge page reinforcing the core value proposition and urgency before the ClickBank form loads
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting checkout-to-purchase from ~5% blended to 15% on 2,296 weekly checkout clicks = ~230 incremental sales × $37 = ~$8,510 additional frontend revenue per week, plus downstream OTO lift

needle_movers.why: The /offer/v4-v3 page achieves only 53.02% offer-to-checkout rate versus 78–86% for other v4 variants, despite 447 offer page views — indicating the CTA placement, copy, or page structure is materially underperforming and costing ~130 checkout clicks per week vs. peers.
what: Compare /offer/v4-v3 CTA button placement, copy, and frequency against /offer/v4/ and /offer/v4-v2 to identify structural differences
Review heatmap or scroll-depth data for /offer/v4-v3 to determine if users are dropping before reaching the primary CTA
A/B test replacing the v4-v3 CTA section with the highest-converting v4 CTA block while keeping the rest of the page copy intact
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting offer-to-checkout from 53% to 80% on 447 weekly offer views = ~121 additional checkout clicks; at current 5.06% purchase rate that is ~6 incremental sales = ~$222/week, with upside if checkout rate also improves

needle_movers.why: OTO2 take rate of 7.5% (8 sales from 106 buyers) is critically below the 15% benchmark, and the OTO2 downsell adds only 3 more sales (2.8%), suggesting the offer or its delivery is broken or the sequence is being skipped for a large proportion of buyers.
what: Verify ClickBank OTO2 redirect URL is correctly configured and firing for all frontend buyers, not just a subset — check for any conditional logic errors in the funnel sequence
Review OTO2 (Timeline) page copy and price point ($47) for relevance to the buyer who just purchased the $37 front-end — test a more congruent bridge or reduced price ($37) as the primary offer
Audit OTO1 → OTO2 flow timing and whether buyers who purchase OTO1 are correctly bypassing to OTO2 or falling out of the sequence
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO2 take rate from 7.5% to 20% on 106 buyers = ~13 incremental OTO2 sales × $47 = ~$611 additional backend revenue per week

needle_movers.why: OTO1 at 25.5% is borderline weak (below the 25–39% average range midpoint), and 68 buyers declined both OTO1 and its downsell this week, representing significant unrealised backend value at $67 and $47 respectively.
what: Test OTO1 page headline and lead copy — ensure the Blueprint offer has a clear, immediate, and congruent benefit bridge from the front-end purchase the buyer just made
Add a video or animated proof element (testimonials, sample pages) to the OTO1 page to increase perceived value before the buy button
Test the OTO1 downsell at $37 instead of $47 to improve downsell capture rate among price-sensitive buyers
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Lifting OTO1 from 25.5% to 35% take rate on 106 buyers = ~10 incremental OTO1 sales × $67 = ~$670 additional revenue per week, with compounding effect on OTO2 exposure

needle_movers.why: The base /offer path shows 319 offer page views but only 3 checkout clicks (0.94%), and the /a landing path (20 views) shows 0 optin completions and unknown offer routing — both indicate either a broken redirect/CTA or untracked traffic segments that may be inflating view counts without commercial intent.
what: Audit the /offer base URL for a broken or missing checkout CTA — compare current live HTML against the working /offer/v4/ version to identify any missing button or JavaScript errors

Investigate the source of 319 /offer page views with only 8 corresponding landing page views — determine if direct traffic, old email links, or referral traffic is landing mid-funnel without proper tracking parameters
Resolve the /a landing path routing so it correctly flows to a tracked offer page, or pause it to avoid polluting aggregate metrics
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Fixing /offer CTA would convert a portion of 316 stranded offer viewers; even a 53% offer-to-checkout rate (v4-v3 baseline) × 5% purchase rate = ~8 incremental sales = ~$296/week currently being lost

cpv_cb_discrepancy.note: CPV and ClickBank purchase counts match exactly at 106; the $216.75 USD revenue difference (4.8%) is within expected range and attributable to OTO postback timing and the SGD-to-USD conversion approximation.

analysis.data_notes: 1) GA4 funnel_v4 shows landing_to_optin_page_pct of 153.1%, which is impossible and indicates a GA4 session inflation or cross-domain tracking issue where the optin page is receiving sessions not originated from the tracked landing page — do not use this ratio for decision-making. 2) The /offer base path flow has a severe mismatch between landing_page_views (8) and offer_page_views (319), suggesting significant direct or mid-funnel traffic arriving at /offer without passing through the tracked landing page, which distorts the offer_to_checkout_rate (0.94%) for that flow. 3) funnel_backend.variant_sales shows 82 of 106 sales attributed to 'other' with no matching funnel_totals breakdown, limiting per-variant revenue analysis — variant-level revenue fields in variant_comparison are set to null. 4) The /a→? and /c/→? flows with zero completions are likely routing or tracking configuration gaps rather than genuine zero-conversion traffic, given the /c/ landing variant performs well in other flows.
